prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 5 minutes long when read aloud.

## Goal
Title: Manage worry about school
Description: Help Noah calm worries about school performance, friendships, and fitting in.

## Person
This is for Noah (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Brief CBT for generalized worry in middle-childhood" (2022)
   Key findings: Worry container technique and belly breathing reduced anxiety scores in 6-10 year olds
   Therapeutic techniques: Worry container visualization; belly breathing; coping self-talk

## Instructions
- Create a complete, flowing therapeutic audio script
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Personalize for Noah (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- Target duration: 5 minutes when read aloud at a calm pace
- Write in English
- Follow the therapeutic audio content structure (warm introduction, understanding the challenge, guided practices, integration)
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any markdown formatting (no **, ##, *, bullet points, or bold/italic syntax). Write plain spoken prose only, as the script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine
script:
Welcome, Noah. I'm so glad you're here with me today. I know sometimes school can feel a little bit tricky, with worries about your work, or your friends, or just wanting to fit in. That's okay. Lots of kids feel that way. Today, we're going to learn some super cool tricks to help those worries feel smaller and quieter. Are you ready? Let's begin.

First, let's get super comfy. You can sit or lie down. Now, let's do some magic belly breathing. Put one hand on your tummy, right on your belly button. Good. Now, imagine your belly is a balloon. When you breathe in slowly through your nose, you're filling the balloon up... one... two... three. Feel your hand rise. Now, let the air out slowly through your mouth, like you're blowing out birthday candles... one... two... three. Feel your hand go down. Let's do that two more times. Breathe in, fill the balloon... and breathe out, slow and steady. One more time. In... and out. Great job. See? You're already a breathing expert.

Now, I want you to imagine something with me. Imagine you have a special box. It can be any kind of box you like. Maybe it's a treasure chest, or a shiny metal box, or a box with your favorite color. This is your Worry Container. It's a really strong box with a good, tight lid. Right now, I want you to picture all those school worries. The thoughts about your work, or your friends. Imagine you're picking up each worry thought, and you're putting it right into the box. Plop. There's one. Plop. There's another. You're the boss of this box. When the worries are inside, you get to close the lid. Maybe you even put a lock on it. Those worries are safe in there for now, so you don't have to think about them this very minute. They can just stay put. How does that feel?

Sometimes, even with the box, a worry might try to pop up. So we need a power phrase. A coping thought. Let's practice one together. You can say it in your mind or whisper it. Ready? Say with me: "I am brave, and I can handle this." Let's try it again. "I am brave, and I can handle this." Awesome. That's your secret power.

So, Noah, you learned some great tools today. You know how to do magic belly breathing to calm your body down. You have your very own Worry Container to put thoughts away for a little while. And you have a brave power phrase to say to yourself. You can use these any time you need them. Remember, it's okay to have worries, and it's also okay to ask for help. You can always talk to a parent, a caregiver, or a teacher you trust. You're doing a really great job. Keep practicing being brave. I believe in you.
